title: Medication Reconciliation Across Care Transitions
subtitle: A 72-year-old is admitted on 11 medications from several prescribers and pharmacies. The nurse performs medication reconciliation — building a complete list, comparing it to new orders, and resolving discrepancies to prevent errors.

patient.name: Arthur Benson
patient.admitDate: Medical unit, admitted with a heart-failure exacerbation. Takes 11 medications prescribed by several providers and filled at two pharmacies, plus an over-the-counter supplement. Nurse performing admission medication reconciliation.

nursesNote: Arthur Benson, 72, admitted with a heart-failure exacerbation on 11 medications from several prescribers and two pharmacies, plus an OTC supplement. Medication reconciliation (a National Patient Safety Goal) builds the most accurate list of ALL medications (prescription, OTC, herbal, supplements, drops, inhalers, patches) from multiple sources (patient/family, pill bottles, pharmacy, records) and compares it to new orders at EVERY transition (admission, transfer, discharge) to find and resolve discrepancies — omissions, duplications, wrong dose/frequency, interactions. Found: an omitted home levothyroxine, a duplicate antihypertensive, and an unreported herbal supplement. Resolve with the provider/pharmacist, then give the patient an updated list with teach-back at discharge and communicate it to the next provider/pharmacy.
providerOrders: Perform medication reconciliation at admission, transfer, and discharge
Obtain a complete medication history from multiple sources (patient, bottles, pharmacy, records)
Include all prescription, OTC, herbal, and supplement use
Identify and resolve discrepancies with the provider and pharmacist
Provide an updated reconciled list with teach-back; communicate to the next provider/pharmacy

opts.text: Medication reconciliation compares the patient's full medication list to new orders
It should include prescription, OTC, herbal, and supplement use
Reconciliation is done at every transition: admission, transfer, and discharge
Transitions of care are high-risk points for medication errors
Only prescription medications need to be reviewed
Multiple sources improve the accuracy of the medication history
Discrepancies include omissions, duplications, and wrong dose or frequency
A "brown-bag" review of the patient's actual bottles helps verify the list
Asking about adherence reveals what the patient actually takes
The pharmacist is a key partner in identifying interactions and duplications
The admission order set can be assumed complete and accurate
At discharge, the patient should receive an updated list with teaching
Gather a complete medication history from multiple sources
Include all prescription, OTC, herbal, and supplement use
Compare the list against the new orders and identify discrepancies
Resolve discrepancies with the provider and pharmacist
Give the patient an updated list with teach-back; communicate it; document

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Does the action support safe reconciliation?

opts.text: Ask about all prescription, OTC, herbal, and supplement use
Use multiple sources (patient, bottles, pharmacy, records)
Compare the home list against new orders and resolve discrepancies
Reconcile at admission, transfer, AND discharge
Give the patient an updated list with teach-back at discharge
Collaborate with the pharmacist on interactions and duplications
Assume the admission order set already lists everything
Skip over-the-counter drugs and supplements
Discharge the patient without a reconciled, updated list

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Interview the patient/family and review pill bottles and pharmacy records
Build the complete list including OTCs and supplements
Reconcile against current orders and flag discrepancies
Collaborate with the provider/pharmacist to resolve them
Provide an updated reconciled list with teach-back and communicate it
A duplicate blood-pressure medication is caught and removed
An omitted home thyroid medication is restarted
The patient gets a clear, updated list and understands the changes
The pharmacy and next provider receive the reconciled list
A home medication is unintentionally omitted and the condition worsens
Two drugs in the same class are continued, causing an overdose effect
An herbal interaction is missed because it wasn't asked about
The patient is confused about which meds to take after discharge
